content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Please speak ___ (quiet) — the baby is sleeping in the next room.
distractor_rationale: the adjective form cannot modify the verb 'speak'; an adverb is required
the comparative adjective form cannot modify a verb; it also requires -ly to become an adverb
'quietful' is not a real word; the correct adverb is formed by adding -ly to 'quiet'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom ran ___ (fast) to catch the bus, but it had already left.
distractor_rationale: 'fastly' is not a real word; 'fast' is already an adverb and needs no -ly suffix
'more fastly' is not a real word; 'fast' does not take -ly in any form
'faster' is the comparative form and does not fit here because no comparison is being made

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: She sings ___ (good) and everyone in the class enjoys her performances.
distractor_rationale: 'good' is an adjective and cannot modify the verb 'sings'; the irregular adverb 'well' is required
'goodly' is not the correct adverb form of 'good'; the irregular form is 'well'
'nicely' changes the meaning; 'well' is the standard adverb used to describe singing ability

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The students listened ___ (careful) while the teacher explained the new grammar rule.
distractor_rationale: the adjective 'careful' cannot modify the verb 'listened'; the adverb 'carefully' is required
'careless' is an adjective with the opposite meaning and cannot modify a verb
'carelessly' means without care and contradicts the context of students paying attention

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Mark works ___ (hard) every evening to finish his project before the deadline.
distractor_rationale: 'hardly' means almost not at all, which completely contradicts working intensively every evening
'hardely' is not a real English word; 'hard' already functions as an adverb
'very hard' adds an unnecessary intensifier that is not cued in the base word 'hard' given in parentheses

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The instructions were ___ (surprising) easy to follow, so we finished the task in ten minutes.
distractor_rationale: the adjective 'surprising' cannot modify another adjective 'easy'; an adverb is required
'surprised' is a past participle / adjective describing a person's feeling, not an adverb modifying 'easy'
'surprise' is a noun or verb and cannot function as a modifier before an adjective

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna ___ (usual) checks her emails before breakfast and then starts her work.
distractor_rationale: the adjective 'usual' cannot function as a frequency adverb before the main verb 'checks'
inserting 'is' creates a different verb structure; the sentence already has the main verb 'checks'
'often usual' combines two separate modifiers incorrectly; only the single adverb 'usually' is needed

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The new manager explained the company's goals ___ (clear) during the meeting, so the whole team understood what was expected.
distractor_rationale: the adjective 'clear' cannot modify the verb 'explained'; the adverb 'clearly' is grammatically required in this position
'in a clear way clearly' is a redundant and ungrammatical combination of two modifiers
'more clearly' is a comparative adverb implying a comparison with another occasion, which is not supported by the context

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The doctor ___ (strong) advised her patient to rest for at least a week and avoid any strenuous activity.
distractor_rationale: the adjective 'strong' cannot modify the verb 'advised'; the adverb 'strongly' is required
'with strength' is a prepositional phrase that collocates with physical actions, not with the verb 'advise'
'strenghtly' is not a real word; the correct adverb derived from 'strong' is 'strongly'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The results of the experiment were ___ (significant) different from what the researchers had predicted at the start.
distractor_rationale: the adjective 'significant' cannot modify another adjective 'different'; an adverb is required before an adjective
'significate' is not a real English word in any grammatical category
'significance' is a noun and cannot function as an adverb modifying the adjective 'different'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom runs very ___ (fast / fastly) and always wins the school race.
distractor_rationale: 'fastly' does not exist in English; 'fast' is already the adverb form and never takes -ly
'faster' is a comparative adjective/adverb and changes the meaning to a comparison, which the sentence does not make
'fast-ly' is not a real word; hyphenated forms of this type do not exist in standard English

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Maria plays the piano ___ (good) for her age, so her teacher is very pleased.
distractor_rationale: 'good' is an adjective and cannot modify the verb 'plays'; the adverb form is 'well'
'goodly' does not exist as a standard adverb in modern English and would be completely unnatural here
'nicely' means pleasantly or attractively and does not collocate with playing a musical instrument at a skill level

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The instructions on the box were ___ (clear) written, so everyone understood what to do.
distractor_rationale: 'clear' is an adjective and cannot modify the past participle 'written'; the adverb 'clearly' is required
'in a clear way' is a prepositional phrase that is grammatically clumsy before a past participle and unnatural in this position
'more clearly' introduces a comparison that has no basis in the sentence, making the meaning illogical

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: She ___ (hard) worked all weekend to finish the project before the Monday deadline.
distractor_rationale: 'hardly worked' means she did almost no work at all, which contradicts the idea of finishing the project before the deadline
'worked hardly' is grammatically incorrect; 'hardly' as a negative adverb of degree cannot follow the verb in this structure
'hard worked' places the adverb before the verb without an auxiliary, which is ungrammatical in standard English for this adverb

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The manager ___ (frequent) reminds the team to double-check their reports before sending them to the client.
distractor_rationale: 'frequent' is an adjective and cannot modify the verb 'reminds'; the adverb 'frequently' is required in this position before the main verb
'is frequently' inserts an auxiliary that breaks the simple present tense structure, making the sentence grammatically incorrect as a standalone clause
'very frequent' uses an adjective intensified by 'very', but an adjective cannot modify a verb; the adverb form 'frequently' is needed

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom runs ___ every morning before school.
distractor_rationale: 'fastly' is a common learner error — 'fast' is already an adverb and does not take -ly
'faster' is a comparative adjective/adverb and changes the meaning without any comparison present
'fastest' is a superlative and cannot be used without a comparison context here

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Maria sings ___ in the school choir every week.
distractor_rationale: 'good' is an adjective and cannot modify the verb 'sings' — a common error of using adjective form instead of adverb
'goodly' does not exist as a standard adverb — learners incorrectly add -ly to 'good'
'niceful' is not a real English word — learners sometimes invent forms by analogy

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Please drive ___ — the roads are wet and dangerous today.
distractor_rationale: 'careful' is an adjective and cannot modify the verb 'drive' — learners often use the adjective form instead of the adverb
'more careful' is a comparative adjective phrase, not an adverb, and no comparison is being made
'care' is a noun or base verb and cannot function as an adverb modifying 'drive'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna ___ checks her email before starting work in the morning.
distractor_rationale: this repeats part of the sentence and makes the whole prompt a run-on — not a valid single-word option
placing 'always' between the verb and its object is incorrect; frequency adverbs go before the main verb, not between verb and object
this puts the frequency adverb after the object and inverts the subject and verb — both errors at once

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The children worked ___ on their art project and finished it before lunch.
distractor_rationale: 'hardly' is a real adverb but means 'almost not at all', which contradicts the result of finishing before lunch
'hardely' is a misspelling that does not exist — learners confuse the irregular adverb 'hard' with a regular -ly form
'hardfully' is not a real English word — a learner error combining 'hard' with a suffix by false analogy

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The instructions were ___ written, so everyone understood what to do.
distractor_rationale: 'clear' is an adjective and cannot modify the past participle 'written' — learners often use the adjective form instead of the adverb
'clarity' is a noun and cannot function as an adverb modifying 'written'
'clearing' is a present participle or noun and has no adverbial function in this context

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: He ___ forgets his keys, so his wife leaves a spare set by the door.
distractor_rationale: placing 'often' between the verb and its object is incorrect; frequency adverbs must precede the main verb
this inverts the verb and object and places the adverb in the wrong position, producing an ungrammatical sentence
this inverts subject and verb and misplaces the adverb, making the sentence completely ungrammatical

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The new assistant completed the report ___ and handed it to her manager before the deadline.
distractor_rationale: 'efficient' is an adjective and cannot modify the verb 'completed' — a learner error of using the adjective form instead of the adverb
'efficiency' is a noun and cannot function as an adverb modifying the verb 'completed'
'more efficiently than' is a comparative adverb phrase requiring a second term for comparison, which is not present in the sentence

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The lecturer spoke ___ quickly that most students struggled to take notes during the talk.
distractor_rationale: 'such' modifies nouns or noun phrases, not adverbs like 'quickly', so 'such quickly' is ungrammatical
'too quickly' means 'excessively fast' and does not introduce the result clause with 'that' in the same way — 'too … to' is the correct pattern, not 'too … that'
'very quickly' intensifies the adverb but cannot introduce a result clause with 'that' — only 'so' creates the 'so … that' result structure

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Despite the noise outside, Lisa ___ concentrated on her work and met every target that week.
distractor_rationale: 'remarkable good' uses an adjective to modify another adjective, which is incorrect — an adverb must modify an adjective or another adverb
'remarkably good' correctly forms the adverb but 'good' is an adjective and cannot modify the verb 'concentrated' — the adverb 'well' is required
'remarkable well' uses the adjective form 'remarkable' instead of the adverb 'remarkably' to modify 'well', which is incorrect

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: She sings beautiful in the school choir every Friday.
content.error_sentence: She sings beautiful in the school choir every Friday.
content.corrected_sentence: She sings beautifully in the school choir every Friday.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: He plays the guitar goodly and practises every day after school.
content.error_sentence: He plays the guitar goodly and practises every day after school.
content.corrected_sentence: He plays the guitar well and practises every day after school.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom drives fastly on the motorway, so his passengers always feel nervous.
content.error_sentence: Tom drives fastly on the motorway, so his passengers always feel nervous.
content.corrected_sentence: Tom drives fast on the motorway, so his passengers always feel nervous.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna speaks fluently French, so she always helps visitors at the information desk.
content.error_sentence: Anna speaks fluently French, so she always helps visitors at the information desk.
content.corrected_sentence: Anna speaks French fluently, so she always helps visitors at the information desk.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The children were playing quiet in the garden while their mother was on the phone.
content.error_sentence: The children were playing quiet in the garden while their mother was on the phone.
content.corrected_sentence: The children were playing quietly in the garden while their mother was on the phone.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: My colleague works hardly to meet every deadline, even when the projects are very difficult.
content.error_sentence: My colleague works hardly to meet every deadline, even when the projects are very difficult.
content.corrected_sentence: My colleague works hard to meet every deadline, even when the projects are very difficult.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The new software update has made the laptop noticeable slower, which is frustrating for everyone in the office.
content.error_sentence: The new software update has made the laptop noticeable slower, which is frustrating for everyone in the office.
content.corrected_sentence: The new software update has made the laptop noticeably slower, which is frustrating for everyone in the office.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: She always carefully checks her report before she sends it to the manager, but she still occasionally makes small errors.
content.error_sentence: She always carefully checks her report before she sends it to the manager, but she still occasionally makes small errors.
content.corrected_sentence: She always checks her report carefully before she sends it to the manager, but she still occasionally makes small errors.
